title: Why Your Stripe MRR Doesn't Match Your Bank Deposits
meta_description: Your Stripe MRR says $12k. Your bank shows $9,847. Here's why the numbers never match — and which one you should actually trust.
h1: Why Your Stripe MRR Doesn't Match Your Bank Deposits (And Which One Is Lying)
body_markdown:
Last March I spent a full Saturday afternoon trying to reconcile $1,847 that Stripe claimed I had earned but my bank had never seen. I had a spreadsheet open, three browser tabs of Stripe reports, a coffee that had gone cold, and a slowly building suspicion that I had been doing accounting wrong for about eighteen months.

The answer, it turned out, was boring. The answer is always boring. But the path to it taught me more about how Stripe actually works than any of their docs had, so I want to save you the Saturday.

If you've ever stared at your Stripe MRR number and then at your Mercury or Chase deposits and thought "these should be closer than this," you're not crazy. They're measuring two completely different things, and nobody at Stripe is incentivized to make that obvious to you.

## The five reasons the numbers will never match

Let me get the mechanical stuff out of the way first, because most posts on this topic stop here and pretend they've answered the question. They haven't. But you do need the mechanics.

MRR is a forward-looking accounting fiction. Bank deposits are backward-looking cash facts. They will never, ever be the same number, and if they ever are it's a coincidence. Here's why:

1. **Stripe fees come out before the deposit.** A $100 charge nets you about $96.80 (2.9% + $0.30, US cards). Your MRR counts the $100. Your bank sees the $96.80. On $10k MRR that's a $320 gap before you've done anything else wrong.
2. **Payout timing is on a rolling 2-day delay** for most US accounts, longer if you're new or international. So a charge on the 31st of the month doesn't hit your bank until the 2nd of the next month, which means your January MRR includes money that arrives in February.
3. **Refunds and disputes claw back from future payouts**, not from the original transaction. A $200 refund issued today reduces your next deposit, but it's accounted against the original month's revenue. Your bank and your MRR drift apart by exactly the refund amount until they reconcile, which they sort of never do cleanly.
4. **Failed renewals.** Stripe's smart retries (they rolled this out properly around 2019 and have been quietly improving the ML behind it since) mean a $49 subscription that fails on the 1st might succeed on the 4th, the 7th, or never. MRR usually still counts that customer as active during the retry window. Your bank, obviously, does not.
5. **Annual plans wreck everything.** A customer who pays $1,200 upfront for a year shows up as $100 MRR (because you're amortizing) but as a $1,200 deposit minus fees. One annual customer can make a single month's bank deposit look 12x your MRR. Or, if nobody renewed annually that month, 0x.

That's the mechanics. Now the interesting part.

## MRR is a story you tell yourself

Here's the thing nobody told me when I started: MRR is not a Stripe number. MRR is a *convention*. Stripe will show you a number called MRR in their dashboard, and it will be different from the MRR ChartMogul shows you, which will be different from what Baremetrics shows you, which will be different from what your spreadsheet says. I have personally watched all four disagree by more than 8% on the same account on the same day.

They disagree because MRR requires you to make decisions, and each tool makes them differently. Do you count a customer in their 14-day trial? Stripe's dashboard does. Baremetrics doesn't, by default. Do you count a customer who's mid-dunning on day 4 of a failed payment? Depends on the tool. Do you normalize an annual plan by dividing by 12, or by the actual number of days? Do you count one-time setup fees? What about a $20 add-on that the customer bought as a separate one-time charge?

Every one of those is a judgment call. And every tool answers them slightly differently, which is why your investor's diligence person is going to ask which methodology you're using and you're going to feel stupid for not knowing. (I felt stupid. It's fine. Now you won't.)

The bank deposit number, on the other hand, is just *what happened*. It's the only number you can't argue with. It's also, conveniently, the number your accountant actually cares about at year-end.

## The reconciliation that actually matters

For a couple years I tried to make MRR and bank deposits match. I'd build spreadsheets. I'd export Stripe's Balance Transactions report (which, by the way, is the actual source of truth for what hit your bank — not the Payments report, not the Charges report, the Balance Transactions report). I'd pivot it by month. I'd compare to deposits. I'd find a $43 discrepancy, hunt it for an hour, discover it was a Stripe Tax remittance from a Q3 update I'd forgotten about, and rage-quit.

I was solving the wrong problem.

The real reconciliation isn't MRR-to-bank. It's *Stripe Balance Transactions to bank deposits*. Those two should match exactly, modulo timing. If they don't, something is genuinely wrong — usually a payout that bounced, a Stripe Tax line item, or a chargeback fee you didn't know about.

MRR is a separate exercise entirely. MRR is for answering questions like "is my growth rate accelerating" and "what's my net revenue retention." It is not for answering "how much money do I have." Conflating those two questions is the original sin.

I got this wrong for about six months in 2022 and made at least one hiring decision based on a confused reading of the two numbers. The hire worked out. The reasoning didn't.

## What to actually look at every Monday morning

My current weekly routine, refined over too many quarters of getting it wrong:

I look at three numbers. New MRR added last week. Churned MRR last week. And cash deposited last week. That's it. The first two tell me if the business is healthy. The third tells me if I can make payroll.

The deposits number I get from Mercury, not from Stripe, because Mercury also shows me the Stripe Tax remittances and the chargebacks as separate line items, which is useful. The MRR numbers I used to get from a Google Sheet that pulled from the Stripe API via a Zapier hack that broke roughly every six weeks. Then I tried Baremetrics — fine product, but $129/mo for the Startup plan felt insane when I was doing $4k MRR myself. ChartMogul has a free tier up to $10k MRR which is more honest pricing for early-stage, but their UI feels like enterprise software designed for a CFO, not for one person trying to answer one question on a Monday.

This is roughly the gap that **Stripe Revenue Tracker** was built to fill — give a solo founder the actual MRR number, with the methodology spelled out, without having to log into Stripe or pay enterprise SaaS prices for a number you check twice a week. It's not magic. It's a calculation Stripe could show you themselves but won't, packaged in a way that doesn't make you feel like you need a finance degree.

The larger point, though, is independent of any tool: stop trying to reconcile MRR to your bank account. They're not the same number. They're not supposed to be. One is a model of your business; the other is the consequence of running it. You need both, but you need to stop expecting them to agree.

And if you're sitting there right now with two browser tabs open trying to figure out where $1,847 went — close them. Open Stripe's Balance Transactions report instead. Filter to the date range of the deposit you're missing. The money is there. It's always there. It's just labeled in a way that took me a Saturday to learn.
cover_image_prompt: Editorial illustration, dark mode, deep navy and charcoal background with muted purple and amber accents. Two abstract geometric shapes — one a rigid grid of glowing rectangles representing structured ledger data, the other a flowing liquid form representing cash flow — meeting at a misaligned seam that doesn't quite connect. Thin gold line traces the gap between them. Minimal, concept-art style reminiscent of Bloomberg Businessweek or The Verge feature illustrations. Subtle grain texture. Asymmetric composition. No text, no people, no logos, no literal dollar signs.
